data.title: Bausch Health Q4 Earnings Recap (Reiterating Buy Rating)
data.description:
In the last quarter, after Bausch Health Companies (NYSE:BHC) posted results, shares entered a steady downtrend. Last week, BHC stock found support at around $6.30. On Thursday, Feb. 19, the out-of-favor firm unexpectedly posted earnings and revenue that beat consensus estimates. Shares are up by over 10% at the time of writing.

In my two previous articles, I rated Bausch Health a stock to buy. The stock returned nearly 26% since last July 2024 and lost around 3% since May. What should investors consider after the fourth-quarter earnings report?

Q4 Results

BHC reported Q4 GAAP earnings per share of $0.25. Revenue increased by 6.2% Y/Y to $2.56 billion. It issued full-year revenue growth of 2.0% to 6.0% and adjusted EBITDA growth of 3.0% to 7.0%.

BHC highlighted achieving seven straight quarters of Y/Y growth in revenue and adjusted non-GAAP EBITDA. Revenue increased by 10% for the year, when excluding foreign exchange ($70 million), acquisitions ($293 million), and divestitures and discontinuations ($77 million). In Q4, revenue increased in all segments except International and Diversified Products. However, excluding the impact of foreign exchange, the International segment revenue increased by 1%.

Overall, shareholders should welcome the consolidated positive net income of $93 million, compared to the $39 million net loss last year.

Upcoming Catalysts

BHC’s litigation around the Food and Drug Administration involving Norwich is a positive catalyst. Chief Executive Officer Thomas Appio said on the conference call that the FDA denied giving Norwich final approval. It concluded that Teva Pharmaceutical Industries (TEVA) did not forfeit its first filer status. Norwich sued the FDA, requesting the agency to grant final approval of its ANDA.

BHC believes that the FDA is correct in its determination of Teva remaining the first filer.

The Norwich case is a positive catalyst since it has an impact on the FDA approval of a generic Xifaxan. With a 30-month stay still applying, BHC may focus on the success of this drug. CEO Appio said that it is prepared to go through the Medicare renegotiation list that comes into effect in 2027. BHC is in a strong position with the drug since it achieves cost savings and benefits.

The CEO said, “In terms of when you look at Xifaxan on the OHE indication, which is more than 70% of the business, the amount of cost savings regarding hospitalizations is -- it drives a big benefit.”

Bausch & Lomb’s discontinuation of the sale process on Feb. 6 is another positive development. B+L said that “after engagement with potential buyers, that process is complete, and will not result in a transaction at this time.” The company is better off realizing higher shareholder returns than selling itself to private banks at a discount. BHC’s EVP and CFO Jean-Jacques Charhon said on the call that it will seek value creation through its BLCO equity stake.

While the CFO has many options available, he did not provide many details. Using the BLCO equity stake as collateral for financing is one of many options. In addition, he set a three-year timeframe for the asset monetization. The timing will depend on BHC’s covenants and obligations related to the debt it plans to raise.

2025 Revenue Growth Catalysts

The CFO expects that Salix, Xifaxan, and Solta will contribute the most to BHC’s growth this year. BHC stock is rallying after the earnings report on expectations that the growth momentum will continue. The firm requires cash flow growth to cover payments for its interest on debt. Fortunately, the debt obligation peaks in 2028. This will give BHC the time it needs to accelerate its long-term growth.

Risks

The risk of U.S. tariffs on trade partners is on most investors’ minds. For BHC, CFO Charhon said that the assumed 25% tariffs would have an impact of less than $50 million in cash flow for the full year.

The Xifaxan price negotiation is another risk. The firm may not get the prices that it wants, hurting its cash flow forecast after January 2027.

Takeaway

BHC stock is among the drug manufacturers that are out of favor. Bigger firms like Merck (MRK), Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY), and Teva Pharmaceutical also traded lower in recent weeks.

The over 10% rally in BHC stock could fade if buying volume weakens in the coming days. For long-term investors uninterested in trading the stock’s volatility, the strong Q4 report is positive news. It reaffirms my “Buy” rating on the company.

data.title: Corporación América Airports: Accelerating Traffic Growth To Power Next Leg Higher
data.description:
Corporación América Airports S.A. (NYSE:CAAP) is one of the world's leading private airport concession holders. It operates 52 airports spanning South America, Europe, and Eurasia.

The company got its start in 1998 when it won the AA2000 concession in Argentina, which gave it the right to manage most of Argentina's leading airports. It controls these airports, including both major Buenos Aires ones, through 2038. It has also since added airport operations in Brazil, Italy, Armenia, Ecuador, and Uruguay. It's also seeking potential expansion opportunities in additional markets, such as Nigeria and Angola.

I have been a proponent of CAAP stock dating back to 2020 when I highlighted that its liquidity position was better than the market was giving it credit for, thus making it likely to survive the global travel shutdown. CAAP stock has since rallied from $2/share at that point to almost $20 per share today.

Even after the huge move, however, I remain convinced that the stock remains substantially undervalued. Here's why I see CAAP stock as a strong buy today.

Strong Traffic Growth And Increased Profitability

I recently highlighted how Mexico's Grupo Aeroportuario del Centro Norte, S.A.B. de C.V. (OMAB) has seen its traffic inflect sharply higher over the past quarter. As recently as November 2024, OMAB had been comping negative traffic year-over-year, but that has now surged to 9.9%-plus as of January 2025. It was understandable to think that this dynamic was somewhat unique to the Mexican market as Mexico's leading carrier, Controladora Vuela Compañía de Aviación, S.A.B. de C.V. (VLRS), was hit especially hard by the Pratt & Whitney engine defects.

However, CAAP is now reporting a similarly vigorous turn in its traffic data. Here's a table showing the company's air traffic by country in January 2025 as compared to January 2024:

Jan'25

Jan'24

% Change

Passenger Traffic (thousands)

Argentina

4,218

3,738

12.8%

Italy

485

459

5.6%

Brazil

1,283

1,497

-14.3%

Uruguay

243

232

4.7%

Ecuador

373

345

8.1%

Armenia

393

368

6.7%

TOTAL

6,995

6,640

5.3%

Source - Corporate press release. Note that Brazil was down because CAAP's Natal airport concession ended in 2024.

I highlight the country-by-country traffic data to show that CAAP is now reporting favorable results in all markets except Brazil, which is unique because it ceased operating the Natal Airport in February 2024. Excluding the Natal lease effect, CAAP's overall traffic grew 9.5% year-over-year. This was up significantly on a sequential basis from the 6.6% increase noted in December and the 0.2% decline that CAAP posted in November, respectively.

Making matters even better, CAAP reported a 13.9% growth rate in international traffic for January, including a stunning 22% rise in international traffic in its Argentine market.

This is vital to profitability as international traffic has higher revenue per passenger than domestic. That's true in general for airport operators as international passengers have a higher propensity to spend on food, retail, gifts and ancillary services such as hotel and car rental. It's specifically even more true for CAAP due to its fee structure in Argentina.

CAAP charges a low rate, typically equal to a couple bucks, for each departing passenger on Argentine domestic flights, and the rate is set in Argentine Pesos, meaning it's subject to dramatic FX volatility. For international passengers departing Argentina, however, CAAP gets to charge a fixed $57 rate, set in U.S. dollars. This is a large part of CAAP's appeal in that it gives investors exposure to the Argentine economic recovery while still having guaranteed U.S. dollar-based revenues that are insulated from potential Peso devaluation.

The Airport Operator's Shares Remain Sharply Undervalued

It's easy to look at a stock that has already enjoyed a huge rally and think that it must be reaching a full valuation.

In some cases, however, fundamentals keep up with the stock price, and shares remain cheap even after a big run. CAAP stock is one such case.

CAAP completed its IPO while trading around 11x EV/EBITDA back in 2018 and shares were trading at or above 8x up until the Argentine economy plummeted in 2019.

CAAP stock was selling for as low as 5x EV/EBITDA during the pandemic, such as in 2022. Surprisingly enough, it remains quite close to its historical low valuation:

How has CAAP stock gone up so dramatically while the EV/EBITDA ratio has remained below 7x?

That's because Corporación America Airports was able to slash its expenses dramatically during the pandemic, and this gave it a better operational footing as the world economy reopened. Argentina, in particular, is known for higher labor costs and elevated overhead compared to other LatAm markets, so the pandemic was a unique opportunity to reduce costs that likely would otherwise have remained bloated.

Add it all up and even though overall traffic is only modestly above 2019 levels, CAAP's EBITDA is already close to 50% higher now than it was at pre-pandemic times:

And now with passenger traffic seemingly on course for a double-digit growth year in 2025, we could be set for a serious upward move in the firm's EBITDA generation capacity this year.

If we suppose that CAAP is worth 10x EV/EBITDA, roughly on par with where it traded post-IPO in 2018, how would that work out? I'd note that in 2018, Argentina had a conservative government, under President Macri, but one which was more moderate in nature than the current Javier Milei-led libertarian government. Under Macri and with a less favorable operating cost structure, CAAP was able to garner a 10x EV/EBITDA multiple.

Adding three turns of EBITDA to CAAP's valuation would require increasing the enterprise value by about $1.8 billion. Meanwhile, CAAP has 161 million shares of stock outstanding. This implies that CAAP stock needs to trade up by about $11/share, giving us a price target of $31 to get to a still modest 10x EV/EBITDA multiple. In the event that CAAP stock trades up to 12x EBITDA, this would suggest a fair value for CAAP stock of about $38 per share.

Again, that's using the firm's current EBITDA. Figure in double-digit traffic growth this year, and we could easily be printing closer to $700 million in annual EBITDA going forward, which would move my fair value targets for CAAP stock into the $40s.

I've spent most of the valuation discussion on EV/EBITDA as it's an industry standard for airport companies. Depreciation is a sizable cost here, and due to the very different cost bases for various airport contracts around the world, GAAP earnings can be less than apples-to-apples comparison between different airport operators.

That said, CAAP stock also trades incredibly cheap on a P/E basis as well. The company has earned $2.16 over the past 12 months, which puts the stock at less than 10x trailing earnings. Earnings have surged dramatically since 2022 and are now far beyond the company's pre-pandemic earnings power.

The Seeking Alpha earnings consensus only has two analysts in it, as CAAP is not a widely followed stock. That said, I'd suggest this set of estimates is conservative:

The down year-over-year estimate for 2024 implies a poor Q4 which seems improbable given the favorable traffic trends we've seen in recent months. And given said traffic growth, it's quite likely that CAAP can exceed $2/share in earnings in 2025 as well. EPS figures can be fairly volatile here due to FX fluctuations and also CAAP's costs associated with expanding its airports and doing work for bidding on potential new concessions.

That said, even on analyst projections shown above, CAAP stock is quite cheap on an earnings basis, especially at 7.9x projected 2026 earnings. And from my perspective, these numbers are a touch too low, and we could easily see them overshoot on the upside.

CAAP Stock Verdict

Let's briefly touch on the positive macroeconomic factors for Corporación America Airports. The travel industry remains strong, and that's particularly true in Latin American markets where the company derives the majority of its revenues.

Argentina is a particularly compelling market right now due to its President, Javier Milei. Milei was the former chief economist at Corporación America, the parent company of the airport operator. Not surprisingly, he has focused on aviation industry policy since becoming president and has taken aggressive measures to deregulate the Argentine airline industry and welcome foreign airline competition. These measures are starting to pay off, as CAAP's recent traffic data indicate.

That said, there's an argument for the idea that investor sentiment is reaching near-euphoric levels around the Argentine equity market and that some issuers, such as Argentine banks, have probably become overvalued. To that point, the Global X MSCI Argentina ETF (ARGT) is up 70% over the past 12 months and more than 200% over the past five years. I have tempered my enthusiasm for Argentine equities, as a whole, given the dramatic rise in valuations in that market over the past 18 months.

However, as I aimed to show in this article, CAAP stock remains compellingly cheap in its own right. And due to so much of its revenues being in U.S. dollars and Euros, CAAP is much less exposed to internal Argentine economic or currency fluctuations than most other Argentine-listed companies.

As such, regardless of your view on Argentina as a country or economy, CAAP stock remains a strong opportunity. If you're bullish on Argentina, and President Milei continues to make major economic reforms in that country, all the better. But even putting that aside, CAAP stock is trading at about 10x earnings and 7x EV/EBITDA today and should be set for double-digit growth in 2025. Shares shouldn't stay on sale below $20 for long given these facts.

data.title: Chimerix: Potential 'First' In Targeting Recurrent High-Grade Glioma Patients
data.description:
Chimerix (NASDAQ:CMRX) announced that the FDA had accepted for review its New Drug Application [NDA] of dordaviprone for the treatment of patients with recurrent H3 K27M-mutant diffuse glioma. Not only was such an application accepted for review by this U.S. agency, but it was done so with Priority Review. That is, the FDA has given this drug for the treatment of this patient population a PDUFA date of August 18th of 2025. There is a lot to be bullish on here, especially given the fact that the FDA doesn't even plan to hold an advisory committee to review this NDA. Other positive factors to take into consideration on why investors should be bullish are a potential Priority Review Voucher for this program, the ability to get the drug launched early in Australia on a Provisional Basis, and expansion towards a first-line H3 K27M-mutant diffuse glioma patient population.

Speaking of which, the phase 3 ACTION study which is combining the use of dordaviprone together with standard frontline radiotherapy, is expected to have an interim overall-survival [OS] data readout in Q3 of 2025. The most important point of all being the fact that there are no FDA approved therapies to treat these specific recurrent high-grade glioma patients. Thus, there is an opportunity for this company to achieve a first in targeting them. In terms of market value, the company notes that even though it is an Orphan indication, there are just over >2,000 eligible patients in the United States alone. This could garner it the ability to receive greater than $1 billion in revenues in the United States alone for it. Last but not least, it is already being proactive in developing a second-generation brain-penetrant ClpP Agonist + DRD2 Antagonist in targeting high-grade gliomas and other types of cancers.

Several Bullish Reasons Brings About Possible Huge Year For Dordaviprone Program

As I noted above, Chimerix is in a very good spot in terms of the development of its drug dordaviprone for the treatment of patients with recurrent H3 K27M-mutant diffuse glioma. The reason why is because the FDA not only accepted its NDA of this drug for the treatment of this patient population, but it also granted it Priority Review. With that said, the U.S. agency is going to review the potential approval of this drug by August 18th of 2025. Another bullish aspect, which is highly encouraging, is that it was noted that for the time being there won't be an advisory committee meeting to discuss this specific regulatory application. Before going over the phase 2 study that brought it to this position to be able to file for such an Accelerated Approval, plus other expected milestones, it is first important to go over what a glioma is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being. A Glioma is characterized as a type of brain cancer that originates or forms in glial cells. These glial cells are important, because they are responsible for surrounding the nerve cells in the brain used for signaling. A problem with gliomas is that they are not only aggressive, but can grow very fast. A problem with this is that the growth of tumor can press on the brain or spinal cord and cause the patient a host of symptoms that they experience. Some symptoms that these patients with gliomas can experience are as follows:

  • Memory loss
  • Vision problems
  • Seizures
  • Headaches
  • Personality Problems

The global brain tumor therapeutics market is expected to grow to $5.28 billion by 2030. This is a very large market opportunity even if only having the company to focus on targeting only glioma patients who express the H3 K27M mutation. Even then, the company notes that the total addressable market is expected to exceed $1 billion in the United States alone [slide 3]. How so? Well, it is believed that in this territory there are roughly >2,000 patients. Not only that, but with no approved therapy being available, it could sell dordaviprone with ultra-orphan drug pricing.

The reason why it was able to file for Accelerated Approval of dordaviprone was because of what it was able to achieve in a phase 2 study targeting these patients with HK K27M. One of the most important measures that was highly positive was something known as Response Assessment in Neuro-Oncology 2.0 [RANO 2.0]. In terms of this efficacy measure, it was noted that there was a RANO objective response rate of 28%. Not only that, but the drug was able to establish a greatly improved median duration of response [mDOR]of 10.4 months and a median time to response of 4.6 months. This positive data achieved not only has placed it in a position to file a NDA to the FDA for potential U.S. marketing approval of the drug, but may also have gained it favor with Australian regulators as well. Besides the fact that dordaviprone was given Orphan Drug Designation for this territory for H3 K27M glioma patients, Chimerix gained alignment with the Therapeutic Goods Administration [TGA] to submit an NDA for Provisional Approval of it for this territory as well.

The hope is that the company is able to ultimately receive U.S. marketing approval of dordaviprone for the treatment of these high-grade glioma patients. Not only would it be a "first" approved treatment option of its kind targeting these patients, but it could actually provide a monetary benefit as well. The reason why is because the FDA granted this drug Rare Pediatric Disease Designation. Why is this designation important, and how can it help it to attain a monetary gain on this basis? With this designation given, if a company ultimately achieves FDA approval, then it is given something known as a Priority Review Voucher or PRV. Such a PRV can be sold to another company for > $100 million for starters. On the other hand, it could also decide to just keep the PRV for itself so that it could eventually speed up the review of another drug candidate from its pipeline. The point here is that the company has flexibility to do what it wants with such a PRV. One such example of how much a company can earn when it is sold would be what happened with a pharmaceutical company by the name of PTC Therapeutics (PTCT). It was able to sell its Priority Review Voucher for $150 million. If the company does receive FDA approval, plus ends up selling the PRV for cash, then it could definitely provide a huge boost to shareholder value.

The final note I want to mention, is the development of dordaviprone for the treatment of patients with H3 K27M mutant glioma patients would be the possible expansion opportunity. The goal for the time being is to obtain Accelerated Approval to market this drug for the treatment of these patients in the United States. However, the company is in the process of evaluating the use of this clinical candidate in the ongoing phase 3 ACTION study for a different patient population. This particular study is expected to recruit up to a total of 450 patients who are to be randomized to receive one of the following dosing regimens:

  • Dordaviprone
  • Dordaviprone + Placebo
  • Placebo

This late-stage study is different in the sense that Chimerix is targeting 1st-line [front-line] H3 K27M mutant high-grade glioma patients instead. Thus, the end-game here is to expand upon the patients that it can go after with its drug dordaviprone. This program is highly ideal because there is a catalyst opportunity that is expected to happen for it during this year. That is, it expects to release interim Overall-Survival [OS] data from this phase 3 ACTION study in Q3 of 2025. Believe it or not, but beyond this, there are several other catalysts relating to this very same late-stage study. The best way to classify this is to denote the intended releases that are expected to happen:

  • First interim Overall-Survival data [Which I denoted above]
  • Progression-Free Survival by RANO-HGG assessment
  • Second interim Overall-Survival data
  • Final Overall-Survival data

The ability to expand to the front-line H3 K27M mutant high-grade glioma patient population is going to be very important. That's because of the ability for it to use dordaviprone to go after the estimated 2,000 brain cancer patients who have this mutation. Not only that, but it is believed that this mutation affects around 5,000 people in the top 7 global markets. The hope is that such a regulatory approval in the United States does happen, because at the moment there are no FDA approved drugs for these recurrent H3 K27M mutant high-grade glioma patients. Thus, the company has an ability to achieve a "first" in targeting these patients and providing them with a therapy that can actually work. A major problem with surgery care is that the tumor is not able to be targeted effectively because of the location. Then, when it comes to radiotherapy, this fails to achieve the intended goal which is to prevent recurrence.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Chimerix had a cash balance sheet of $152.4 million as of September 30th of 2024. This is a decent amount of cash on hand in order to fund its pipeline and also possibly initiate commercial launch of its drug dordaviprone for the treatment of patients with recurrent H3 K27M mutant high-grade gliomas. However, in order to make sure that it does have the intended cash for this commercial effort, it had to establish a loan with Silicon Valley Bank. In particular, it entered into and amended and restated loan and security agreement for up to $30 million with this entity. It is key to note though that no draws have been made upon this loan agreement as of yet. What do I mean by this? Well, under the terms of the agreement established, the company can draw upon $20 million from now until all the way through the period ending February 28th of 2026.

From there, it could then choose to draw upon an additional $10 million by the company's own request through February 28th of 2027 [However, this particular $10 million draw is subject to SVB's approval]. The company is going to need to draw upon this loan or enact another financial transaction to continue to fund its operations. The reason why is because of what is actually stated in the 10-Q SEC Filing, which is that the company believes that it only has enough cash on hand to fund its operations for at least the next 12 months. This company burns roughly $24.06 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $18.96 million in R&D expenses and then $5.1 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Chimerix. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing Priority Review of dordaviprone for the treatment of patients with recurrent H3 K27M mutant high-grade gliomas, with a PDUFA target action date of August 18th of 2025. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the company is going to receive U.S. Accelerated Approval of this drug to treat these recurrent brain cancer patients. Not only would this be a huge negative on the stock price itself if such a regulatory approval doesn't happen, but then that would eliminate its ability to obtain a Priority Review Voucher. Thus, it would not be entitled to either keep it to use to speed up the review of another drug or sell it to obtain $100 million or more in cash.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the expansion opportunity that might be possible with the development of dordaviprone for the treatment of these H3 K27M mutant high-grade glioma patients. That's because the established PDUFA date above is only in targeting recurrent patients. Only the phase 3 ACTION study is going to ultimately allow the company to expand its scope into using this drug to target the front-line patient population. The first risk here is that there is no assurance that the company will be able to obtain a positive interim OS data readout from this ongoing phase 3 ACTION study for starters. Secondly, even if this and all other subsequent data readouts I highlighted above are positive, there is no assurance that the company will ultimately receive U.S. marketing approval in targeting front-line H3 K27M mutant high-grade glioma patients.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the development of ONC206, which is a second-generation ClpP Agonist + DRD2 Antagonist in targeting high-grade gliomas and other types of cancers. A recommended phase 2 dose [RP2D] is expected to be reached in the early part of 2025 and thus far, there have been no dose-limiting toxicities to make note of. The risk here is that despite no safety issues or dose-limiting toxicities being noted thus far, this doesn't mean that such problems won't arise in the future. The second risk to consider would be in terms of dordaviprone showing monotherapy in non-clinical Central Nervous System [CNS] models, as well as other tumors outside of the CNS. The risk here is that even though this is observed in preclinical testing, there is no assurance that ONC206 will see similar positive data when in the clinic.

Conclusion

Chimerix has done well in regard to being able to develop its lead pipeline candidate dodraviprone for the treatment of patients with recurrent H3 K27M mutant high-grade glioma patients. Especially since the FDA not only accepted the NDA of this drug for the treatment of patients, but granted it Priority Review with a PDUFA action date of August 18th of 2025. The amount of catalyst opportunities here just with this candidate alone are endless. Ranging from the expected Q3 of 2025 interim data from the phase 3 ACTION study targeting patients with front-line H3 K27M high-grade glioma, to potentially filing Provisional approval of this drug for Australia as well. With a PDUFA date to review the potential approval of dordaviprone for the treatment of patients with recurrent H3 K27M mutant high-grade glioma set for August 18th of 2025, plus the Q3 of 2025 interim OS data readout targeting front-line patients, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.

data.title: Alibaba: Beating Earnings And Firing On All Cylinders
data.description:
Alibaba Group Holding Limited (NYSE:BABA) just reported its earnings for the quarter ended December 2024. The fiscal Q3 release easily surpassed analyst expectations. Revenue came in at $38 billion, up 8%, in line with analyst expectations. GAAP earnings per share (EPS) came in at $2.79, up 333%, ahead of analyst expectations by $0.62. Overall, it was a satisfactory release. Particularly encouraging in the release was the cloud segment’s performance, which saw revenue increase by 13% driven by strong AI adoption–on the post-earnings call, management said that they expected growth in the cloud segment to continue accelerating.

When I last covered Alibaba, I rated the stock a strong buy because it had achieved a new growth catalyst in the form of its widely publicized Qwen 2.5 Max AI Model, which beat DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) V3 on several metrics. With Alibaba’s December quarter earnings release, we can see the BABA’s AI models have, in fact, been driving growth in the company’s cloud business, which has an immediate path to monetizing said models.

So, I remain as optimistic about Alibaba’s prospects as ever. However, with BABA stock having run up 34% since my last article about it was published, I feel it is time to temper my enthusiasm slightly. The stock is more richly valued now than it was when I last wrote about it. In the ensuing paragraphs, I will elaborate on my BABA thesis in more detail.

Earnings Recap

Alibaba’s December earnings release was a major beat on most metrics. Highlights included:

  • $38.38 billion in revenue, up 8% (in line with expectations).

  • $2.79 in earnings per share/ADS, up 333% (beat by $0.62)

  • $2.93 in adjusted EPS, up 6%, beat by $0.27.

  • $5.3 billion in free cash flow, down 31% ((miss)).

  • $4.3 billion in cloud segment revenue, up 13%.

  • $430 million in cloud segment EBITA, up 33%.

  • $5.1 billion in international segment revenue, up 32%.

The results broadly beat analyst expectations, with the three headline metrics (revenue, reported earnings and adjusted earnings) having exceeded the estimates.

BABA’s overall results in the December quarter were strong. The free cash flow performance was a little disappointing but, in light of the major uses of cash in the quarter, it may have been justifiable. Expenses related to growing the international segment and the cloud segment increased in the quarter. These two segments are BABA’s biggest growth drivers, so investing in them makes sense.

Speaking of the cloud segment, it absolutely stole the show in the December quarter. In the quarter, Alibaba Cloud’s revenue growth accelerated to 13%. The growth rate nearly doubled from the prior quarter, which saw 7% growth. The cause of this growth was pretty clear: increasing AI adoption from Chinese companies. Alibaba’s Qwen AI model garnered significant publicity last month when it outperformed DeepSeek-V3 in several benchmarks. This likely inspired companies to sign up for Alibaba Cloud. Although Qwen is available for free on the internet, users need to have Alibaba Cloud accounts to integrate it with their digital services. With Alibaba Cloud, companies can seamlessly integrate Qwen with their operations, using it to create customer service chatbots, launch marketing campaigns and analyze data. These use cases provide a powerful selling point for Alibaba Cloud adoption, as the free online version of Qwen can only be used for end-user purposes (i.e., creating content and analyzing publicly available data).

As far as non-cloud segments go, their results were overall pretty encouraging. International commerce was the fastest growing segment, with overall revenue up 32% and international retail revenue up 35%. EBITA earnings in the segment declined massively, though as I outlined above, that was for a good reason: heavy investment in promoting the segment. AliExpress has been outcompeted by TEMU in several key markets such as the US, so investing in that business is a good idea, even if it leads to weak segment earnings in the near term.

Long-Term Considerations

Having explored BABA’s recent earnings release, it is now time to look at the company’s long-term factors. These include its competitive position, profit metrics and growth.

Competitive Position

Alibaba has a strong competitive position, being #1 in the Chinese cloud market segment, a leader in international wholesale e-commerce, and a strong player in international retail e-commerce. Its cloud segment is China’s largest and most popular; the success of the Qwen AI model is a catalyst that provides a plausible reason to believe this will remain the case going forward.

Alibaba.com, the international wholesale segment, is almost without competition. Amazon B2B does a similar thing (selling bulk goods and large equipment) but is in a different price category than Alibaba. There is one smaller Chinese firm called DHGate that does wholesale sales from China, but it is minimal compared to Alibaba, thus not exercising a large amount of competitive pressure on it.

Alibaba’s China retail segment is its weakest in a competitive sense. It faces competition from JD.com (JD), PDD Holdings (PDD) and even ByteDance (BDNCE). The growth rate in the segment has decelerated over time due to competition from these players. This is something of a problem for Alibaba, as this comparatively weak segment is also the company’s largest. However, if the acceleration in the cloud segment continues, then it will eventually eclipse China retail commerce as a growth driver for the company.

Recent Performance

Alibaba’s recent performance has been relatively strong. In the past 12 months, it grew its revenue by 5%, while its earnings and FCF declined. The decline in earnings and FCF may look concerning, but remember that the recent quarter reversed the trend on the earnings front, while the FCF decline is due to necessary investments that probably won’t recur indefinitely. On a 10-year compounded annual (CAGR) basis, Alibaba grew its revenue by 31% and its earnings by 12%. See the image below for these metrics over other timeframes.

Alibaba scores pretty well on profitability. In the TTM period, it had a 38% gross margin, a 9% net margin, a 9% FCF margin, and an 8.6% return on equity (ROE). These margins aren’t quite as high as they were in the past, due to heavy investments in international growth and cloud infrastructure. However, they remain satisfactory.

Valuation

Having looked at Alibaba’s recent earnings, competitive position and long-term performance, it is time to value the stock.

As illustrated by the image below, Alibaba is quite cheap for a tech stock, trading at 14.5 times earnings, 2.25 times sales and two times book.

The multiples here are low, though not as low as they were in the past. This is the main reason why I downgraded BABA to “buy” from “strong buy”: the company is cheap, but trending less cheap over time.

What Is Alibaba Stock Worth In Objective Terms?

I think there is a case to be made that the company can grow its FCF by 10% over the next five years, and perhaps by 3% in perpetuity after that. Its TTM FCF per share was $6 according to Seeking Alpha Quant. Based on these assumptions, and using an 8% discount rate, BABA stock has an intrinsic value of around $167. So, it has a significant upside.

The Bottom Line

The bottom line on Alibaba stock is that it just delivered its best earnings performance in years, has several growth catalysts, and remains fairly cheap. On the whole, I am quite bullish on this stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Anywhere Real Estate: Debt Challenges Overshadow Positive Q4 Performance
data.description:
Introduction

Anywhere Real Estate Inc. (NYSE:HOUS) is a provider of integrated services for the residential housing industry. The company’s services include real estate franchising, brokerage, relocation, and title and settlement services, while also participating in minority-owned mortgage and title insurance underwriting joint ventures. Through its portfolio of companies, it owns some recognizable brands in the industry, including Better Homes and Gardens Real Estate, Century 21, Coldwell Banker, Corcoran, ERA, and Sotheby’s International Realty. With a network of 182k independent sales agents in the U.S. and 132k agents across another 120+ countries, the company supports over a million home transactions annually.

In terms of the revenue breakdown, the bulk of the company’s sales (around 80%) comes from gross commission income, earned when its affiliated agents facilitate property sales and purchases, though a significant portion of this is paid out to the agents themselves. The company also earns service revenue by providing critical support services for real estate transactions, such as title, escrow, and settlement services. As well, Anywhere Real Estate collects franchise fees from its extensive network of franchisees who operate under its well-known brands and benefit from the company’s marketing, training, and technological resources.

Despite its scale, holding a 12% market share in the U.S., Anywhere has faced profitability challenges in recent quarters, with a net loss of nearly $100 million in 2024. In this article, I’ll discuss the company’s most recent earnings and why, despite improving financials, I’d still avoid the stock today.

Recent Results Are Encouraging

Anywhere Real Estate reported Q4 2024 results that we would characterize as solid. Revenues for the quarter came in at $1.36 billion, up 9% year over year and surpassing the sell-side estimate by $81.6 million. Operating EBITDA clocked in at $52.0 million, an 86% surge compared to the previous year, ahead of Bloomberg consensus estimates for $45.1 million. However, the company’s EPS of -$0.44 missed expectations by 15 cents. This had to do with how the company is now defining ‘Operating EBITDA’, which makes adjustments for non-cash stock compensation and certain legal matters (to be more consistent with how peers report). Adjusting for this, EPS likely wouldn’t have missed as badly.

When looking at what drove results this quarter, total transaction volumes were up 13%, coming from higher home pricing (up 9%). On the conference call, the company noted that transaction volumes were also driven by continued strength in the luxury segment with the luxury brands of Coldwell Banker Global Luxury, Corcoran, and Sotheby’s outperforming the market with a closed transaction volume of 20% year over year in Q4 2024. Commission splits for the quarter stood at 80.3%, down 7bps compared to last year. In my view, I think it’s encouraging to see the stability that the company has been seeing on this stat for several quarters now. While time will tell, it seems that the importance of profitability by competitors in the marketplace is starting to stem the tide of agents leaning on splits.

On commission rates, the franchise business had an average broker commission rate of 2.39%, with the owned brokerage rate having an average commission rate of 2.35%. These figures were down 6 and 7bps year over year, respectively. Overall, I’m not too surprised to see a decrease and expect further compression on the buy side. On the earnings call, management noted that out of the five different buyer agreements they rolled out, buyers continue to favor the six-month exclusive agreement (about 80% of the time). These agreements are sticking close to where buy-side commission rates were prior to the settlement changes, with buyers making up any gap from sellers not offering compensation. To the extent this practice holds into the busy selling season, I think commission rates can remain at these levels but are likely to level off in the medium to long term.

Outlook

Looking to 2025, Anywhere Real Estate pointed to expecting an Operating EBITDA of $350 million. The guidance includes the definition change, but regardless, the company is pointing to improvements in its business. With an operating EBITDA of $350 million, the company is guiding for 20.6% growth in EBITDA and is guiding for $100 million of cost savings, after about $125 million of savings in 2024.

This is important in two ways. Firstly, the company seems to be achieving this without “cutting into muscle” at the organization. It is leveraging the use of generative AI and other tech tools to transform many functions throughout the company. Secondly, there’s more to go. On potential M&A, management noted that it is seeing more opportunities for consolidation across brokerage, adjacent services, and prop tech and that they’re well positioned to explore these opportunities due to their liquidity.

However, one concern that I have with respect to Anywhere Real Estate (especially given the talk around potential M&A) is on the balance sheet, where leverage continues to be high. On the conference call, management noted that they’ll have $115 million in expected one-time free cash flow headwinds in 2025 including an $83.5 million litigation settlement, a $40 million legacy tax matter, and a $20 million settlement. Given these near-term obligations, I have skepticism around the cash position, especially with total debt at $2.61 billion, nearly five times larger than the market capitalization.

Given that the company has a leverage of 7.2x, I would be especially cautious around the company’s future interest payments. As shown below, most of the company’s debt trades well below par, with yield to maturities of 10%+. This suggests to me that there’s a lack of confidence by bondholders with respect to credit risk and with spreads as high as 700bps on some unsecured bonds, it’s likely that a refinancing would mean significantly higher interest rates at some point in time.

With Debt-to-EV sitting around 85%, the small steps the company has been making to reduce their debt, like through the Term Loan A repayment might not be enough. At the very least, it’ll put a significant strain on the company's financial flexibility. If commission rates continue to moderate and intense competition in the industry persists, there’s a risk that the company could struggle to generate sufficient cash flow to meet its debt obligations. Not to mention that the company’s financial performance is inextricably linked to the health of the housing market. Any significant downturn in the real estate sector, whether due to rising interest rates, economic recession, or other macroeconomic factors, could severely impact transaction volumes and revenue. While we aren’t there yet, this is a risk I think investors need to contend with. At this point, I think investors will need to see strong cost-cutting measures and incremental improvements to margins in order for the stock to work. M&A is the last thing the company needs at this point, at least in my view.

Valuation

Most analysts are pretty bearish on the near-term outlook for Anywhere Real Estate’s stock. With six Wall Street analysts covering the stock, three have ‘hold’ ratings while three have ‘sell’ ratings. With an average price target of $3.93, most see the stock as being overvalued with a -2.2% return potential over the next one year (Source: Bloomberg).

For me, I’d rate Anywhere Real Estate as a ‘sell’. While I think there were some positive takeaways from the quarter, like a rising top line and more of a focus on permanent cost-saving measures, I think the company is doing this from a position of weakness, rather than a position of strength. Specifically, as it relates to the balance sheet, I think as those maturities come due, it’ll become increasingly challenging to stay free cash flow positive.

At the current valuation of 8.3x EV/EBITDA, Anywhere Real Estate isn’t exactly a bargain. For a company in a highly competitive industry, brands only matter so much and competition is fierce. Relative to peers, the company trades at a 1.7 turn discount on EV/EBITDA, which I’m surprised isn’t higher, frankly. Given what I would consider to be higher interest rates on the horizon for the company as well as a very serious threat to the business in the event of a recession, I’d be inclined to avoid the company for now.

Conclusion

Ultimately, the market responded positively to Anywhere Real Estate’s Q4 2024 results with promising signs from growing revenues, improved EBITDA, and strong performance in the luxury segment. However, the company’s issues, particularly with its high leverage and the potential risks tied to its significant debt obligations, make it a risky and speculative investment. The company's high Debt to Equity ratio and the looming cash flow headwinds in 2025 present risks outside my comfort zone, with little margin of safety if things go wrong with a volatile housing market. So although cost-saving measures and technological improvements offer some hope, the company’s financial health is vulnerable in my view. For now, given the uncertainty surrounding the housing market and its balance sheet, I’m rating the stock as a ‘sell’.
